title: Introduction
description: Learn what Cerememory is and how it gives AI systems persistent, living memory.
content: What is Cerememory? Cerememory is a living memory database for the age of AI. Built in Rust, it provides persistent, brain-inspired memory that any LLM can use -- regardless of provider. Memories stored in Cerememory are not static rows in a table; they decay, evolve, form associations, and respond to emotion, just as biological memory does. The Problem: LLM Amnesia Every time an LLM conversation ends, everything learned is lost. Context windows are finite and ephemeral. This creates a fundamental limitation for AI applications that need to remember users, learn from past interactions, or build long-term understanding. Current workarounds -- RAG databases, vector stores, chat history logs -- treat memory as a search problem. They return exact copies of stored text ranked by similarity. Biological memory works nothing like this. It is reconstructive, associative, and alive. Core Principles Alive Memories in Cerememory are living entities. They decay over time following a power-law model, accumulate noise, and can be reactivated through retrieval. Emotional memories persist longer. Frequently accessed memories grow more stable. This is not a feature -- it is the foundation. LLM-Agnostic Cerememory works with any LLM provider through the Cerememory Protocol (CMP). Dedicated adapters for Claude, GPT, and Gemini serialize memory context into each model's preferred format, but the core engine is completely provider-independent. User-Sovereign All memory data belongs to the user. Cerememory runs on your infrastructure, stores data locally using redb (an embedded key-value store), and supports encrypted export/import via CMA archives. There is no cloud dependency, no telemetry, and no vendor lock-in. Brain-Inspired Design Cerememory models its architecture after the human brain's memory systems: | Store | Brain Analog | Purpose | |-------|-------------|---------| | Episodic | Hippocampus | Personal experiences and events | | Semantic | Neocortex | Facts, knowledge, and concepts | | Procedural | Basal Ganglia | Skills, workflows, and how-to knowledge | | Emotional | Amygdala | Emotionally significant memories | | Working | Prefrontal Cortex | Active context and short-term memory | Each store has distinct characteristics for decay rates, consolidation behavior, and retrieval patterns. The hippocampal coordinator manages cross-store operations like spreading activation and memory consolidation. What Can You Do With Cerememory? - Store memories with automatic store classification, embedding generation, and association inference - Recall memories with human-like fidelity (noise, decay) or perfect retrieval mode - Traverse association graphs across all memory stores via spreading activation - Run background decay ticks that naturally age and prune stale memories - Consolidate episodic memories into semantic knowledge over time - Export and import encrypted memory archives for portability - Integrate with any LLM via HTTP, gRPC, or MCP transports Next Steps

title: Installation
description: Install Cerememory via cargo, Docker, pip, or npm.
content: Requirements - Rust 1.88+ (for building from source) - No external database required -- Cerememory uses embedded redb for storage Installation Methods Install directly from crates.io: This compiles the binary with default features (all LLM adapters enabled). To build with only specific adapters: Pull the official Docker image: Run with a persistent data volume: Install the Python SDK (includes the server binary): Or install only the client library: Install the TypeScript/JavaScript SDK: Or with yarn: Build from Source The binary will be located at target/release/cerememory. Cerememory requires Rust edition 2021 with resolver v2. The workspace includes over 20 crates -- an incremental build after the first compile is fast. Feature Flags The CLI binary supports feature flags for LLM provider adapters: | Feature | Description | Default | |---------|-------------|---------| | llm-openai | OpenAI / GPT adapter | Enabled | | llm-claude | Anthropic / Claude adapter | Enabled | | llm-gemini | Google / Gemini adapter | Enabled | Disable unused adapters to reduce binary size and compile time. Verify Installation You should see output like: Next Steps

title: Quick Start
description: Store, recall, and forget your first memory with Cerememory.
content: This guide walks you through the basic Cerememory workflow: starting the server, storing a memory, recalling it, and forgetting it. Start the Server Output: The server is now running with default settings: in-memory working store, persistent redb-backed stores for episodic/semantic/procedural/emotional, and background decay ticks every hour. Store a Memory Output: Recall Memories In human mode, recalled content is filtered through fidelity and noise -- older or less-accessed memories may be slightly distorted, just like biological recall. Use perfect mode when exact reproduction is needed. Forget a Memory The --confirm flag is required for all forget operations. Forgetting is permanent and cannot be undone. Use --cascade to also delete associated records. Check System Stats Next Steps

title: Architecture Overview
description: Understand Cerememory's layered architecture and crate structure.
content: System Overview Cerememory is organized as a layered system where each layer has a single responsibility. Requests flow from external clients through transport bindings, into the engine, and down to the individual memory stores. Crate Structure The codebase is organized as a Cargo workspace with crates separated by responsibility. Layer Responsibilities Transport Layer Three transport bindings expose the CMP protocol to external clients: - HTTP/REST (axum) -- Primary transport on port 8420. Includes Bearer token auth, CORS, rate limiting, request tracing, and Prometheus metrics. - gRPC (tonic) -- Optional transport with TLS support. Enabled by setting grpc.port in configuration. - MCP (rmcp) -- Model Context Protocol server over stdio. Enables direct integration with MCP-compatible AI agents. Engine Layer The CerememoryEngine is the central coordinator. It implements all CMP operations (encode, recall, lifecycle, introspect) and orchestrates cross-store interactions. It manages: - The hippocampal coordinator for cross-store recall and spreading activation - The decay engine for background fidelity decay (runs on a configurable interval) - The text index (tantivy) and vector index (HNSW) for fast retrieval - An optional LLM provider for embedding generation, summarization, and relation extraction Store Layer Five specialized stores, each backed by redb (except working memory which is in-memory only). All stores implement the Store trait, providing a uniform interface for CRUD operations, text search, and fidelity updates. Supporting Engines - Decay Engine -- Stateless, rayon-parallel computation of power-law decay across all records - Association Engine -- Spreading activation over the cross-store association graph - Evolution Engine -- Adaptive parameter tuning based on usage patterns Dependency Direction cerememory-core is the leaf dependency -- it defines all shared types, traits, and protocol structures. Every other crate depends on it, but it depends on no internal crate. Next Steps

title: Five Memory Stores
description: Detailed explanation of Cerememory's five brain-inspired memory stores.
content: Overview Cerememory implements five specialized memory stores, each modeled after a distinct region of the human brain. Every store implements the same Store trait, but each has unique characteristics for what it stores, how fast memories decay, and how they interact with other stores. Store Comparison | Store | Brain Analog | Storage | Decay Rate | Consolidation | Use Case | |-------|-------------|---------|-----------|---------------|----------| | Episodic | Hippocampus | redb (disk) | Medium | Migrates to Semantic | Conversations, events, experiences | | Semantic | Neocortex | redb (disk) | Slow | Compresses over time | Facts, knowledge, user preferences | | Procedural | Basal Ganglia | redb (disk) | Very slow | Reinforced by repetition | Workflows, skills, how-to patterns | | Emotional | Amygdala | redb (disk) | Slow (emotion-anchored) | Strengthens associations | Emotionally significant moments | | Working | Prefrontal Cortex | In-memory | Fast | Promotes to Episodic | Active context, current session | Episodic Store (Hippocampus) The episodic store holds personal experiences and events -- what happened, when, and in what context. This is the default destination for new memories when no store is explicitly specified. Key characteristics: - Medium decay rate -- Episodic memories fade at a moderate pace unless reinforced through recall - Rich context -- Stores temporal, spatial, and session context alongside content - Consolidation source -- During consolidation, episodic memories that meet age and access thresholds are migrated to the semantic store as distilled knowledge - Association-rich -- Temporal and sequential associations are automatically created between episodic memories stored in the same session Semantic Store (Neocortex) The semantic store holds facts, knowledge, and conceptual understanding -- the "what" rather than the "when." Semantic memories are distilled from repeated episodic experiences or stored directly as factual knowledge. Key characteristics: - Slow decay rate -- Knowledge persists much longer than episodic memories - Consolidation target -- Receives distilled memories from the episodic store during consolidation - Compression -- Over time, related semantic memories are compressed and summarized - Relation extraction -- When an LLM provider is configured, semantic memories undergo relation extraction to build a knowledge graph Procedural Store (Basal Ganglia) The procedural store holds skills, workflows, and habitual patterns -- the "how." These memories represent learned behaviors that become more stable with repetition. Key characteristics: - Very slow decay -- Procedural memories are among the most persistent - Repetition reinforcement -- Each time a procedural memory is accessed, its stability increases significantly - Structured content -- Often stores structured data (JSON) representing workflows or step-by-step procedures Emotional Store (Amygdala) The emotional store holds emotionally significant memories. The emotional dimension uses Plutchik's 8-dimensional model to represent affective state. Key characteristics: - Emotion-anchored decay -- Higher emotional intensity leads to slower decay via the emotional modulation factor - Cross-store influence -- Emotional tags on any memory (in any store) modulate that memory's decay rate - Valence tracking -- Each emotion has a valence from -1.0 (negative) to +1.0 (positive) The eight primary emotions and

title: Living Dynamics
description: How memories decay, accumulate noise, respond to emotion, and evolve over time.
content: Overview Cerememory's defining feature is that memories are alive. They change over time through six mechanisms: decay, noise accumulation, emotional modulation, reactivation, reconsolidation, and consolidation. This page documents the mathematical models behind each. Fidelity Decay (Power-Law Model) Memory fidelity decreases over time following a modified power-law formula: | Parameter | Symbol | Default | Description | |-----------|--------|---------|-------------| | Initial fidelity | F_0 | 1.0 | Starting fidelity score (0.0 to 1.0) | | Elapsed time | t | -- | Seconds since last access or last decay tick | | Stability | S | 1.0 | Stability constant; increases with each retrieval | | Decay exponent | d | 0.3 | Controls decay speed (per-record, stored in decay_rate) | | Emotional modulation | E_mod | 1.0 | Scales fidelity; > 1.0 for emotional memories | The power-law curve means memories decay rapidly at first and then slowly approach zero -- never quite disappearing, but becoming increasingly unreliable. This matches empirical findings in cognitive science. The decay engine is stateless and processes records in parallel using rayon. Each record's decay is computed independently, making the engine safe for concurrent use and efficient on multi-core machines. Noise Accumulation As fidelity drops, memories accumulate noise -- random distortions that represent interference from other memories: | Parameter | Default | Description | |-----------|---------|-------------| | N_0 | 0.0 | Initial noise level | | interference_rate | 0.1 | Rate of noise accumulation | Key properties: - Noise grows with the square root of time (sub-linear, bounded) - Noise is inversely proportional to fidelity -- more decayed memories are noisier - Both fidelity and noise are clamped to the [0.0, 1.0] interval - In human recall mode, noise is applied to retrieved content; in perfect mode, it is bypassed Emotional Modulation Emotional intensity modulates the decay rate through the E_mod factor: The EmotionVector uses Plutchik's 8-dimensional model with an overall intensity field (0.0 to 1.0) and a valence field (-1.0 to +1.0). At maximum emotional intensity (1.0), E_mod = 1.5, meaning the effective fidelity is 50% higher than a neutral memory at the same age. This causes emotional memories to persist significantly longer. Reactivation (Retrieval Boost) When a memory is recalled, its stability constant increases, making future decay slower: | Parameter | Default | Description | |-----------|---------|-------------| | retrieval_boost | 1.5 | Multiplicative boost factor | This implements a spaced repetition effect: the more a memory is accessed, the more stable it becomes. The boost diminishes with higher stability (the S^(-0.2) term), modeling the psychological observation that well-established memories benefit less from additional rehearsal. Reconsolidation When a memory is recalled (and reconsolidate: true is set in the query), the engine: 1. Increments the record's access_count 2. Updates last_accessed_at to the current timestamp 3. Boosts the stability constant via the retrieval boost formula 4. Persists these changes back to the store This means every recall operation strengthens the retrieved memory, just as biological reconsolidation does. Memories that are never recalled will naturally decay and eventually be pruned.

title: Data Model
description: Core data structures: MemoryRecord, FidelityState, EmotionVector, and more.
content: MemoryRecord The fundamental unit of storage in Cerememory. Every memory -- regardless of which store it resides in -- is a MemoryRecord. Record IDs use UUIDv7, which embeds a timestamp prefix. This ensures IDs are both globally unique and naturally time-ordered, enabling efficient range scans. StoreType Identifies which memory store a record belongs to: MemoryContent The payload of a memory record. Content is organized as a list of modality-typed blocks, supporting multimodal memories. Supported modalities: | Modality | Format Examples | Max Size | Notes | |----------|----------------|----------|-------| | text | text/plain | 1 MB | Must be valid UTF-8 | | image | image/png, image/jpeg | 10 MB | Auto-detected format | | audio | audio/wav, audio/mp3 | 50 MB | Auto-detected format | | video | video/mp4 | 50 MB | -- | | structured | application/json | 1 MB | Must be valid JSON | | spatial | application/json | 1 MB | Location/coordinate data | | temporal | application/json | 1 MB | Time-series data | | interoceptive | application/json | 1 MB | Internal state data | FidelityState Tracks the current decay state of a memory record. This is the core data structure that makes memories "alive." Default values for a new record: | Field | Default | Description | |-------|---------|-------------| | score | 1.0 | Fresh memories start at full fidelity | | noise_level | 0.0 | No noise initially | | decay_rate | 0.3 | Standard power-law exponent | | emotional_anchor | 1.0 | Neutral (no emotional boost) | | stability | 1.0 | Base stability | | reinforcement_count | 0 | Never recalled | EmotionVector Multi-dimensional affective representation based on Plutchik's wheel of emotions. Emotion vectors can be created from string labels for convenience: Supported labels: joy (or happy/happiness), trust, fear, surprise, sadness (or sad), disgust, anger (or angry), anticipation (or anticipatory). Association A weighted, typed, bidirectional link between two memory records. Associations enable the spreading activation engine to traverse the memory graph. RecallMode Controls whether recall applies living dynamics or returns pristine data: Serialization All data types support both JSON and MessagePack serialization: MessagePack is used internally for storage in redb. JSON is used for HTTP transport and CMA archive files. Both formats round-trip losslessly. Next Steps

title: CMP Protocol Overview
description: Introduction to the Cerememory Protocol (CMP) -- the transport-agnostic API for living memory.
content: What is CMP? The Cerememory Protocol (CMP) is a versioned, transport-agnostic protocol that defines all operations for interacting with Cerememory's living memory system. CMP messages can be carried over HTTP/REST, gRPC, MCP, or any future transport binding. Every CMP message includes a protocol header with versioning: The header is optional in requests -- the server generates one if omitted. Version compatibility follows semver: any 1.x version is compatible with 1.0. Four Operation Categories CMP organizes all operations into four categories: | Category | Prefix | Purpose | |----------|--------|---------| | Encode | encode. | Store and update memories | | Recall | recall. | Query and retrieve memories | | Lifecycle | lifecycle. | Manage memory dynamics (decay, consolidation, forget) | | Introspect | introspect. | Observe system state and memory metadata | Operation Summary Encode (CMP Spec Section 3) | Operation | Description | |-----------|-------------| | encode.store | Store a new memory record | | encode.batch | Store multiple records with optional association inference | | encode.update | Update an existing record's content, emotion, or metadata | Recall (CMP Spec Section 4) | Operation | Description | |-----------|-------------| | recall.query | Multimodal cue-based retrieval with Human/Perfect modes | | recall.associate | Traverse the association graph from a given record | | recall.timeline | Time-bucketed retrieval within a date range | | recall.graph | Retrieve a subgraph of memory nodes and edges | Lifecycle (CMP Spec Section 5) | Operation | Description | |-----------|-------------| | lifecycle.consolidate | Migrate mature episodic memories to semantic store | | lifecycle.decay_tick | Manually trigger a decay computation cycle | | lifecycle.set_mode | Switch between Human and Perfect recall modes | | lifecycle.forget | Permanently delete records (requires confirmation) | | lifecycle.export | Export records to a CMA archive | | lifecycle.import | Import records from a CMA archive | Introspect (CMP Spec Section 6) | Operation | Description | |-----------|-------------| | introspect.stats | System-wide statistics (record counts, avg fidelity, etc.) | | introspect.record | Detailed view of a single record | | introspect.decay_forecast | Predict future fidelity for given records | | introspect.evolution | View adaptive parameter adjustments | Error Handling (CMP Spec Section 7) All errors are returned as a standardized CMPError envelope: Error codes: | Code | HTTP Status | Description | |------|-------------|-------------| | RECORD_NOT_FOUND | 404 | Record does not exist | | STORE_INVALID | 400 | Invalid store name | | CONTENT_TOO_LARGE | 413 | Content exceeds size limit | | VALIDATION_ERROR | 400 | Request validation failed | | MODALITY_UNSUPPORTED | 400 | Modality not supported by provider | | WORKING_MEMORY_FULL | 429 | Working memory at capacity | | DECAY_ENGINE_BUSY | 503 | Decay computation in progress | | CONSOLIDATION_IN_PROGRESS | 409 | Consolidation already running | | FORGET_UNCONFIRMED | 400 | Missing confirm: true | | VERSION_MISMATCH | 400 | Optimistic concurrency conflict | | UNAUTHORIZED | 401 | Invalid or missing API key | | RATE_LIMITED | 429 | Too many requests (includes retry_after) | | INTERNAL_ERROR

title: Encode Operations
description: Store and update memories using CMP encode operations.
content: encode.store Store a new memory record. This is the primary way to add memories to Cerememory. Endpoint: POST /v1/encode Request | Field | Type | Required | Default | Description | |-------|------|----------|---------|-------------| | content | MemoryContent | Yes | -- | The memory payload (blocks + optional summary) | | store | StoreType | No | Engine auto-selects | Target store (episodic, semantic, procedural, emotional, working) | | emotion | EmotionVector | No | Neutral | Emotional context for this memory | | context | EncodeContext | No | None | Source, session, spatial, and temporal context | | associations | ManualAssociation[] | No | None | Explicit associations to existing records | Response When store is omitted, the engine selects the most appropriate store based on content analysis. Text that looks like a fact goes to semantic; narratives go to episodic; step-by-step instructions go to procedural. encode.batch Store multiple records in a single request with optional automatic association inference between the batch members. Endpoint: POST /v1/encode/batch Request | Field | Type | Required | Default | Description | |-------|------|----------|---------|-------------| | records | EncodeStoreRequest[] | Yes | -- | Array of individual store requests | | infer_associations | bool | No | false | Automatically create associations between batch members | Response When infer_associations is true, the engine creates temporal and sequential associations between consecutive records in the batch. If an LLM provider is configured, semantic associations are also inferred. encode.update Update an existing record's content, emotion, or metadata. Uses optimistic concurrency control via the record's version field. Endpoint: PATCH /v1/encode/:record_id Request | Field | Type | Required | Description | |-------|------|----------|-------------| | content | MemoryContent | No | Replace content entirely | | emotion | EmotionVector | No | Replace emotion vector | | metadata | JSON | No | Replace metadata object | All fields are optional -- only provided fields are updated. The record's updated_at timestamp and version are automatically incremented. Response Returns 204 No Content on success. Updating a record does not reset its fidelity or decay state. The memory continues to age from its original creation time. If you need to "refresh" a memory, recall it (which triggers reconsolidation) rather than updating it. Next Steps

title: Recall Operations
description: Query and retrieve memories using multimodal cues, association traversal, timelines, and graphs.
content: recall.query The primary retrieval operation. Accepts a multimodal cue and returns ranked memories with relevance scores. Endpoint: POST /v1/recall/query Request | Field | Type | Required | Default | Description | |-------|------|----------|---------|-------------| | cue | RecallCue | Yes | -- | Multimodal query cue | | stores | StoreType[] | No | All stores | Filter to specific stores | | limit | u32 | No | 10 | Maximum results to return | | min_fidelity | f64 | No | None | Exclude records below this fidelity | | include_decayed | bool | No | false | Include records below prune threshold | | reconsolidate | bool | No | true | Strengthen retrieved memories | | activation_depth | u32 | No | 2 | Hops for spreading activation | | recall_mode | RecallMode | No | human | "human" (with noise) or "perfect" (exact) | Recall Cue The RecallCue supports multiple modalities simultaneously: Response In human mode, rendered_content may differ from the original record.content -- noise is applied based on the memory's current fidelity and noise level. In perfect mode, they are identical. recall.associate Traverse the association graph starting from a specific record. Returns memories connected by typed edges. Endpoint: POST /v1/recall/associate/:record_id Request | Field | Type | Required | Default | Description | |-------|------|----------|---------|-------------| | association_types | AssociationType[] | No | All types | Filter by association type | | depth | u32 | No | 2 | Maximum traversal depth | | min_weight | f64 | No | 0.1 | Minimum edge weight to follow | | limit | u32 | No | 10 | Maximum results | Response recall.timeline Retrieve memories organized into time buckets within a date range. Endpoint: POST /v1/recall/timeline Request | Field | Type | Required | Default | Description | |-------|------|----------|---------|-------------| | range | TemporalRange | Yes | -- | Start and end timestamps | | granularity | TimeGranularity | No | hour | minute, hour, day, week, month | | min_fidelity | f64 | No | None | Fidelity floor | | emotion_filter | EmotionVector | No | None | Filter by emotional similarity | Response recall.graph Retrieve a subgraph of memory nodes and their association edges, suitable for visualization. Endpoint: POST /v1/recall/graph Request Response Next Steps

title: Lifecycle Operations
description: Manage memory dynamics: consolidation, decay, mode switching, forgetting, export, and import.
content: lifecycle.consolidate Trigger memory consolidation -- migrating mature episodic memories into the semantic store as distilled knowledge. Endpoint: POST /v1/lifecycle/consolidate Request | Field | Type | Required | Default | Description | |-------|------|----------|---------|-------------| | strategy | ConsolidationStrategy | No | incremental | full, incremental, or selective | | min_age_hours | u32 | No | 0 | Minimum age of episodic records to consider | | min_access_count | u32 | No | 0 | Minimum access count to qualify | | dry_run | bool | No | false | Preview without making changes | Response Use dry_run: true to preview which records would be consolidated without making any changes. This is useful for tuning the min_age_hours and min_access_count thresholds. lifecycle.decay_tick Manually trigger a decay computation cycle. In server mode, this runs automatically in the background, but you can trigger it explicitly for testing or manual control. Endpoint: POST /v1/lifecycle/decay-tick Request | Field | Type | Required | Default | Description | |-------|------|----------|---------|-------------| | tick_duration_seconds | u32 | No | None | Simulated time to add (for testing) | Response The decay tick processes all records across all persistent stores in parallel. Records whose fidelity falls below the prune threshold (default: 0.01) are automatically deleted. lifecycle.set_mode Switch the global recall mode between Human and Perfect. Endpoint: PUT /v1/lifecycle/mode Request | Field | Type | Required | Default | Description | |-------|------|----------|---------|-------------| | mode | RecallMode | Yes | -- | human or perfect | | scope | StoreType[] | No | All stores | Limit mode change to specific stores | Response Returns 204 No Content on success. lifecycle.forget Permanently delete memory records. Requires explicit confirmation to prevent accidental data loss. Endpoint: DELETE /v1/lifecycle/forget Request | Field | Type | Required | Default | Description | |-------|------|----------|---------|-------------| | record_ids | Uuid[] | No | None | Specific records to delete | | store | StoreType | No | None | Delete all records in a store | | temporal_range | TemporalRange | No | None | Delete records within a time range | | cascade | bool | No | false | Also delete associated records | | confirm | bool | Yes | -- | Must be true to proceed | Response Forget is irreversible. Without confirm: true, the server returns a FORGET_UNCONFIRMED error. With cascade: true, all records linked by associations are also deleted. lifecycle.export Export memory records to a CMA (Cerememory Archive) file for backup or portability. Endpoint: POST /v1/lifecycle/export (via CLI: cerememory export) Request | Field | Type | Required | Default | Description | |-------|------|----------|---------|-------------| | format | string | No | cma | Archive format | | stores | StoreType[] | No | All stores | Filter to specific stores | | encrypt | bool | No | false | Encrypt the archive with ChaCha20-Poly1305 | | encryption_key | string | No | None | Base64-encoded encryption key | Response lifecycle.import Import memory records from a CMA archive. Request | Field | Type | Required | Default

title: Introspect Operations
description: Observe system state, inspect records, forecast decay, and monitor evolution.
content: introspect.stats Retrieve system-wide statistics including record counts, average fidelity, and background decay status. Endpoint: GET /v1/introspect/stats Response | Field | Type | Description | |-------|------|-------------| | total_records | u32 | Total records across all stores | | records_by_store | Map | Record count per store | | total_associations | u32 | Total association edges | | avg_fidelity | f64 | Average fidelity score across all records | | avg_fidelity_by_store | Map | Average fidelity per store | | oldest_record | DateTime | Timestamp of the oldest record | | newest_record | DateTime | Timestamp of the newest record | | total_recall_count | u64 | Total recall operations performed | | background_decay_enabled | bool | Whether background decay is running | introspect.record Inspect a specific memory record with full detail including fidelity state, associations, and access history. Endpoint: GET /v1/introspect/record/:record_id Request Parameters | Parameter | Type | Description | |-----------|------|-------------| | record_id | UUID (path) | The record to inspect | The following query parameters are available for the CMP-level request: | Field | Type | Default | Description | |-------|------|---------|-------------| | include_history | bool | false | Include access history | | include_associations | bool | false | Include association details | | include_versions | bool | false | Include version history | Response Returns the full MemoryRecord as JSON: introspect.decay_forecast Predict the future fidelity of specific records at a given point in time. Endpoint: POST /v1/introspect/decay-forecast Request | Field | Type | Required | Description | |-------|------|----------|-------------| | record_ids | Uuid[] | Yes | Records to forecast | | forecast_at | DateTime | Yes | Target date for the forecast | Response The estimated_threshold_date is the projected date when fidelity will drop below the prune threshold. A null value means the memory is projected to remain above threshold indefinitely (high stability or emotional anchoring). introspect.evolution View the evolution engine's adaptive parameter adjustments, detected usage patterns, and schema adaptations. Endpoint: GET /v1/introspect/evolution Response The evolution engine is optional and runs only when sufficient usage data has been collected. Early in a Cerememory instance's lifecycle, this endpoint may return empty arrays. Next Steps

title: Python SDK
description: Complete reference for the Cerememory Python SDK with sync and async clients.
content: Installation For the native PyO3-based bindings (no HTTP overhead): Sync Client store Store a new memory record. | Parameter | Type | Required | Default | Description | |-----------|------|----------|---------|-------------| | text | str | Yes | -- | Text content to store | | store | str | No | auto | Target store | | emotion | str or dict | No | None | Emotion label or EmotionVector dict | | embedding | list[float] | No | None | Pre-computed embedding vector | | metadata | dict | No | None | Arbitrary metadata | | session_id | str | No | None | Session identifier for context | recall Query memories by text cue. | Parameter | Type | Required | Default | Description | |-----------|------|----------|---------|-------------| | text | str | Yes | -- | Text query | | limit | int | No | 10 | Maximum results | | mode | str | No | "human" | "human" or "perfect" | | stores | list[str] | No | All | Filter by store | | min_fidelity | float | No | None | Minimum fidelity threshold | | reconsolidate | bool | No | True | Strengthen retrieved memories | forget Delete records permanently. stats Get system statistics. inspect Get full details for a specific record. decay_tick Trigger a manual decay cycle. consolidate Trigger memory consolidation. Async Client For asyncio-based applications: The async client provides the same methods as the sync client, but all return coroutines. Error Handling All CMP error codes are mapped to typed exceptions: | Exception | CMP Code | |-----------|----------| | RecordNotFoundError | RECORD_NOT_FOUND | | ValidationError | VALIDATION_ERROR | | ContentTooLargeError | CONTENT_TOO_LARGE | | WorkingMemoryFullError | WORKING_MEMORY_FULL | | RateLimitedError | RATE_LIMITED | | UnauthorizedError | UNAUTHORIZED | | CerememoryError | All others | Next Steps

title: TypeScript SDK
description: Complete reference for the Cerememory TypeScript/JavaScript SDK.
content: Installation Or with yarn: Client Setup Methods store Store a new memory record. | Parameter | Type | Required | Default | Description | |-----------|------|----------|---------|-------------| | text | string | Yes | -- | Text content | | store | StoreType | No | auto | Target store | | emotion | string or EmotionVector | No | undefined | Emotion label or vector | | embedding | number[] | No | undefined | Pre-computed embedding | | metadata | Record | No | undefined | Arbitrary metadata | | sessionId | string | No | undefined | Session context | storeBatch Store multiple records in a single request. recall Query memories with a text cue. | Parameter | Type | Required | Default | Description | |-----------|------|----------|---------|-------------| | text | string | Yes | -- | Text query cue | | limit | number | No | 10 | Maximum results | | mode | "human" or "perfect" | No | "human" | Recall mode | | stores | StoreType[] | No | all | Filter by store | | minFidelity | number | No | undefined | Minimum fidelity | | reconsolidate | boolean | No | true | Strengthen recalled memories | | activationDepth | number | No | 2 | Association traversal depth | associate Traverse the association graph from a specific record. forget Permanently delete records. stats Get system statistics. inspect Get full details for a specific record. decayTick Trigger a manual decay cycle. consolidate Trigger memory consolidation. Error Handling Type Definitions The SDK exports full TypeScript types for all CMP structures: Next Steps

title: Native Bindings
description: Zero-overhead PyO3 and napi-rs native bindings for Python and Node.js.
content: Overview In addition to the HTTP-based SDKs, Cerememory provides native bindings that embed the Rust engine directly in your Python or Node.js process. These bindings eliminate network overhead and provide the fastest possible access to the memory engine. Native bindings are built using: - PyO3 for Python -- compiles to a native Python extension module - napi-rs for Node.js -- compiles to a native Node.js addon Native bindings run the Cerememory engine in-process. There is no HTTP server involved. This is ideal for single-process applications, testing, and high-throughput scenarios. Python Native (PyO3) Installation The cerememory package includes pre-built wheels for Linux (x86_64, aarch64) and macOS (x86_64, aarch64). If no pre-built wheel is available, pip will compile from source (requires Rust toolchain). Usage API Differences from HTTP Client The native API is slightly different from the HTTP client: | HTTP Client | Native Binding | Notes | |-------------|---------------|-------| | client.store(text=...) | engine.store(text=...) | Same signature | | client.recall(text=...) | engine.recall(text=...) | Returns list, not wrapper object | | client.forget(record_ids=[...], confirm=True) | engine.forget(record_ids=[...]) | Confirm not required (no accidental HTTP calls) | | client.stats() | engine.stats() | Same structure | | Async client available | Always sync (GIL released) | Use asyncio.to_thread for async | The native binding releases the Python GIL during Rust operations, so it is safe to use from multiple threads. For asyncio applications, wrap calls with asyncio.to_thread(). Node.js Native (napi-rs) Installation Pre-built binaries are included for Linux (x86_64, aarch64), macOS (x86_64, aarch64), and Windows (x86_64). Usage napi-rs Features The napi-rs binding provides: - Async/await support -- All operations return Promises and run on the Rust tokio runtime - Serde JSON bridging -- Automatic conversion between Rust types and JavaScript objects - TypeScript types -- Full .d.ts type definitions included When to Use Native Bindings | Scenario | Recommended | |----------|-------------| | Embedded in a single application | Native bindings | | Multiple clients sharing one memory store | HTTP server | | Testing and development | Native bindings (simpler setup) | | Production with health checks and metrics | HTTP server | | High-throughput batch processing | Native bindings | | Multi-language polyglot architecture | HTTP server | Building from Source Python Node.js Next Steps

title: Configuration
description: Configure Cerememory via TOML file, environment variables, and CLI flags.
content: Configuration Precedence Cerememory loads configuration from three sources, in order of precedence (highest wins): 1. Environment variables -- CEREMEMORY_ prefix with __ separator 2. TOML configuration file -- Specified via --config flag 3. Hard-coded defaults -- Sensible defaults for all settings TOML Configuration File Create a cerememory.toml file: Pass the config file to the CLI: Environment Variables All configuration options can be overridden via environment variables with the CEREMEMORY_ prefix. Nested keys use __ (double underscore) as the separator. | Environment Variable | Config Key | Default | Description | |---------------------|-----------|---------|-------------| | CEREMEMORY_DATA_DIR | data_dir | ./data | Data directory path | | CEREMEMORY_HTTP__PORT | http.port | 8420 | HTTP server port | | CEREMEMORY_HTTP__CORS_ORIGINS | http.cors_origins | [] | Allowed CORS origins | | CEREMEMORY_GRPC__PORT | grpc.port | None | gRPC port (disabled if unset) | | CEREMEMORY_GRPC__TLS_CERT_PATH | grpc.tls_cert_path | None | TLS certificate path | | CEREMEMORY_GRPC__TLS_KEY_PATH | grpc.tls_key_path | None | TLS key path | | CEREMEMORY_AUTH__ENABLED | auth.enabled | false | Enable Bearer token auth | | CEREMEMORY_AUTH_API_KEYS | auth.api_keys_raw | [] | Comma-separated API keys | | CEREMEMORY_LLM__PROVIDER | llm.provider | none | LLM provider name | | CEREMEMORY_LLM__API_KEY_RAW | llm.api_key_raw | None | LLM API key | | CEREMEMORY_LLM__MODEL | llm.model | None | Model name override | | CEREMEMORY_LLM__BASE_URL | llm.base_url | None | API base URL override | | CEREMEMORY_DECAY__BACKGROUND_INTERVAL_SECS | decay.background_interval_secs | 3600 | Decay tick interval (seconds) | | CEREMEMORY_RATE_LIMIT__REQUESTS_PER_SECOND | rate_limit.requests_per_second | 100 | Rate limit RPS | | CEREMEMORY_RATE_LIMIT__BURST | rate_limit.burst | 50 | Rate limit burst | | CEREMEMORY_LOG__LEVEL | log.level | info | Log level | | CEREMEMORY_LOG__FORMAT | log.format | pretty | Log format | CEREMEMORY_AUTH_API_KEYS is a special case -- it accepts a comma-separated string since environment variables cannot natively represent arrays. Example: CEREMEMORY_AUTH_API_KEYS=key1,key2,key3 CLI Flag Overrides CLI flags take the highest precedence for the specific options they cover: | Flag | Overrides | |------|-----------| | --port | http.port | | --grpc-port | grpc.port | | --data-dir | data_dir | | --config | Path to TOML config file | Validation The configuration is validated at startup. The following rules are enforced: - HTTP port must be non-zero - gRPC port (if set) must be non-zero and different from HTTP port - If auth is enabled, at least one API key must be configured - TLS cert and key must be provided together (both or neither) - Rate limit values must be greater than zero API keys and LLM keys are stored as SecretString at runtime to prevent accidental logging. The Debug implementation for AuthConfig and LlmConfig redacts sensitive values. Data Directory Structure When Cerememory starts, it creates the following structure inside data_dir: Next Steps

title: Docker
description: Run Cerememory with Docker and Docker Compose.
content: Quick Start This starts Cerememory with default settings: HTTP on port 8420, data persisted in a Docker volume, and background decay enabled. Docker Run with Full Configuration Docker Compose Store sensitive values like API keys in a .env file or a secrets manager. The Docker Compose file above references ${CEREMEMORY_API_KEY} and ${ANTHROPIC_API_KEY} from the environment. Health Checks Cerememory exposes two health endpoints that do not require authentication: | Endpoint | Purpose | Success Response | |----------|---------|-----------------| | GET /health | Liveness probe | {"status": "ok"} (200) | | GET /readiness | Readiness probe | {"status": "ready"} (200) | The /readiness endpoint performs an actual engine operation (reading stats) and returns 503 if the engine is not ready. For Docker's HEALTHCHECK, use the built-in CLI command: Building the Image To build from source: Volumes and Data Persistence | Volume Mount | Purpose | Required | |-------------|---------|----------| | /data | Persistent storage (redb files, indexes) | Yes | | /etc/cerememory/cerememory.toml | Config file (read-only) | No | Always mount a persistent volume at /data. Without it, all memories are lost when the container restarts. The working memory store is always in-memory and is intentionally not persisted. Metrics Prometheus metrics are available at GET /metrics (no authentication required) when the server is started. Metrics include: - cerememory_http_requests_total -- Total HTTP requests by method and status - cerememory_http_request_duration_seconds -- Request latency histogram - cerememory_records_total -- Total records by store - cerememory_decay_ticks_total -- Total background decay ticks Next Steps

title: Kubernetes
description: Deploy Cerememory to Kubernetes with probes, persistent storage, and scaling.
content: Deployment Manifest PersistentVolumeClaim Cerememory uses redb, an embedded database that requires ReadWriteOnce access. Do not use ReadWriteMany as concurrent access to the same redb file from multiple pods will cause data corruption. Service ConfigMap Secrets In production, use a secrets manager (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, etc.) instead of Kubernetes Secrets. The example above is for demonstration purposes. Probes Cerememory provides three probe endpoints: | Probe | Endpoint | Behavior | |-------|----------|----------| | Liveness | GET /health | Returns 200 if the process is alive | | Readiness | GET /readiness | Returns 200 if the engine can serve requests; 503 otherwise | | Startup | GET /health | Same as liveness; use a higher failureThreshold | Both /health and /readiness bypass authentication and rate limiting, so they work even when auth is enabled. Scaling Considerations Cerememory uses an embedded database (redb), which means: - Horizontal scaling is not supported -- Each replica needs its own data volume. Memories are not shared between replicas. - Vertical scaling works well -- The decay engine uses rayon for parallel computation and benefits from additional CPU cores. - Read replicas are not available -- Use a single replica with appropriate resource limits. For high-availability scenarios, consider: 1. Running a single replica with a restart policy 2. Using PersistentVolumeClaims with a durable storage class 3. Regular CMA archive exports for backup Prometheus ServiceMonitor If you use the Prometheus Operator: Next Steps

title: API Endpoints
description: Complete HTTP REST endpoint reference for the Cerememory Protocol.
content: Base URL All API endpoints are prefixed with /v1/. Health and metrics endpoints are at the root level. Authentication When auth.enabled = true, all /v1/* endpoints require a Bearer token in the Authorization header: Health endpoints (/health, /readiness) and metrics (/metrics) bypass authentication. Endpoint Reference Health & Metrics | Method | Path | Auth | Description | |--------|------|------|-------------| | GET | /health | No | Liveness probe -- returns {"status": "ok"} | | GET | /readiness | No | Readiness probe -- verifies engine is operational | | GET | /metrics | No | Prometheus metrics (text format) | Encode | Method | Path | Auth | Request Body | Response | |--------|------|------|-------------|----------| | POST | /v1/encode | Yes | EncodeStoreRequest | EncodeStoreResponse (200) | | POST | /v1/encode/batch | Yes | EncodeBatchRequest | EncodeBatchResponse (200) | | PATCH | /v1/encode/:record_id | Yes | EncodeUpdateRequest | 204 No Content | Recall | Method | Path | Auth | Request Body | Response | |--------|------|------|-------------|----------| | POST | /v1/recall/query | Yes | RecallQueryRequest | RecallQueryResponse (200) | | POST | /v1/recall/associate/:record_id | Yes | RecallAssociateRequest | RecallAssociateResponse (200) | | POST | /v1/recall/timeline | Yes | RecallTimelineRequest | RecallTimelineResponse (200) | | POST | /v1/recall/graph | Yes | RecallGraphRequest | RecallGraphResponse (200) | Lifecycle | Method | Path | Auth | Request Body | Response | |--------|------|------|-------------|----------| | POST | /v1/lifecycle/consolidate | Yes | ConsolidateRequest | ConsolidateResponse (200) | | POST | /v1/lifecycle/decay-tick | Yes | DecayTickRequest | DecayTickResponse (200) | | PUT | /v1/lifecycle/mode | Yes | SetModeRequest | 204 No Content | | DELETE | /v1/lifecycle/forget | Yes | ForgetRequest | {"records_deleted": N} (200) | Introspect | Method | Path | Auth | Request Body | Response | |--------|------|------|-------------|----------| | GET | /v1/introspect/stats | Yes | -- | StatsResponse (200) | | GET | /v1/introspect/record/:record_id | Yes | -- | MemoryRecord (200) | | POST | /v1/introspect/decay-forecast | Yes | DecayForecastRequest | DecayForecastResponse (200) | | GET | /v1/introspect/evolution | Yes | -- | EvolutionMetrics (200) | Error Responses All errors return a CMPError JSON envelope: HTTP Status Code Mapping | CMP Error Code | HTTP Status | Description | |---------------|-------------|-------------| | RECORD_NOT_FOUND | 404 | Record does not exist | | STORE_INVALID | 400 | Unknown store name | | CONTENT_TOO_LARGE | 413 | Exceeds modality size limit | | VALIDATION_ERROR | 400 | Malformed request or invalid values | | MODALITY_UNSUPPORTED | 400 | Provider does not support this modality | | WORKING_MEMORY_FULL | 429 | Working memory capacity exceeded | | DECAY_ENGINE_BUSY | 503 | Decay tick already in progress | | CONSOLIDATION_IN_PROGRESS | 409 | Consolidation already running | | EXPORT_FAILED | 500 | Archive export failed | | IMPORT_CONFLICT | 409 | Import conflict detected | | FORGET_UNCONFIRMED | 400 | Missing confirm: true | | VERSION_MISMATCH | 400 | Optimistic concurrency conflict | | UNAUTHORIZED | 401 | Missing or invalid API key | | RATE_LIMITED | 429 | Rate limit exceeded (includes

title: CLI Reference
description: Complete reference for all Cerememory CLI commands and flags.
content: Global Flags | Flag | Type | Default | Description | |------|------|---------|-------------| | --data-dir | path | ./data | Data directory for persistent storage | | --config | path | None | Path to TOML configuration file | --- serve Start the HTTP server (and optionally gRPC). | Flag | Type | Default | Description | |------|------|---------|-------------| | --port | u16 | 8420 | HTTP port | | --grpc-port | u16 | None | gRPC port (disabled if not set) | The server starts with: - Background decay enabled (configurable interval) - Graceful shutdown on SIGINT/SIGTERM - Prometheus metrics at /metrics - Health probes at /health and /readiness --- store Store a new memory record. | Flag | Type | Default | Description | |------|------|---------|-------------| | ` | string | -- | Required. Text content to store | | --store | string | episodic | Target store (episodic, semantic, procedural, emotional, working) | | --embedding | string | None | Comma-separated embedding vector (e.g., "0.1,0.2,0.3") | Output: Storing to the working store prints a warning: working memory is in-memory only and will not persist across restarts. --- recall Recall memories by text query. | Flag | Type | Default | Description | |------|------|---------|-------------| | | string | -- | Required. Text query | | --limit | u32 | 10 | Maximum results | | --mode | string | human | Recall mode: "human" or "perfect" | Output: --- inspect Inspect a specific record with full detail. | Flag | Type | Description | |------|------|-------------| | | UUID | Required. Record UUID to inspect | Outputs the full MemoryRecord as pretty-printed JSON, including fidelity state, emotion vector, associations, and metadata. --- stats Display system-wide statistics. Outputs JSON with total records, per-store counts, average fidelity, total associations, and recall count. --- decay-tick Manually trigger a decay computation cycle. | Flag | Type | Default | Description | |------|------|---------|-------------| | --duration | u32 | 3600 | Simulated duration in seconds | Output: --- forget Permanently delete a memory record. | Flag | Type | Default | Description | |------|------|---------|-------------| | | UUID | -- | Required. Record to delete | | --cascade | bool | false | Also delete associated records | | --confirm | bool | false | Required confirmation flag | The --confirm flag is mandatory. Without it, the operation is rejected. This prevents accidental deletion. --- export Export all records to a CMA archive file. | Flag | Type | Default | Description | |------|------|---------|-------------| | --output | path | ./backup.cma | Output file path | --- import Import records from a CMA archive file. | Flag | Type | Description | |------|------|-------------| | | path | Required. Path to the CMA archive file | --- healthcheck Lightweight HTTP health check (useful for Docker HEALTHCHECK). | Flag | Type | Default | Description | |------|------|---------|-------------| | --port` | u16 | 8420 | HTTP port to check | Returns exit code 0 on success, non-zero on failure. --- mcp Start the

title: LLM Adapters
description: Configure and use Claude, GPT, and Gemini adapters for embedding, summarization, and relation extraction.
content: Overview Cerememory integrates with LLM providers through two trait-based interfaces: - LLMProvider -- Handles embedding generation, summarization, and relation extraction - LLMAdapter -- Serializes memory context into LLM-consumable format LLM integration is optional. Without a configured provider, Cerememory falls back to manual embeddings, truncation-based summarization, and skips relation extraction. Supported Providers | Provider | Config Value | Crate | Features | |----------|-------------|-------|----------| | Anthropic Claude | claude or anthropic | adapter-claude | Text embedding, summarization, relation extraction, image embedding (vision) | | OpenAI GPT | openai | adapter-openai | Text embedding, summarization, relation extraction, image embedding, audio transcription | | Google Gemini | gemini or google | adapter-gemini | Text embedding, summarization, relation extraction, image embedding | Configuration Environment variables: Environment variables: Environment variables: Provider Capabilities Each provider advertises its capabilities via the ProviderCapabilities struct: | Capability | Claude | OpenAI | Gemini | No Provider | |------------|--------|--------|--------|-------------| | Text embedding | Yes | Yes | Yes | No | | Image embedding | Yes (vision) | Yes (vision) | Yes (vision) | No | | Audio transcription | No | Yes (Whisper) | No | No | | Summarization | Yes | Yes | Yes | Truncation fallback | | Relation extraction | Yes | Yes | Yes | Skipped | LLMProvider Trait The provider trait defines the core LLM operations: LLMAdapter Trait The adapter trait handles context serialization for LLM consumption: Each adapter serializes memories optimally for its target model, respecting the model's context window and token limits. Fallback Behavior When no LLM provider is configured (provider = "none"): | Operation | Fallback Behavior | |-----------|------------------| | Embedding generation | Returns empty vector (manual embeddings required) | | Summarization | Truncates to first 200 characters | | Relation extraction | Returns empty list (no graph enrichment) | | Image embedding | Returns ModalityUnsupported error | | Audio transcription | Returns ModalityUnsupported error | The NoOp provider is used internally when provider = "none". It satisfies the LLMProvider trait but performs no API calls. Cerememory is fully functional without an LLM -- you lose automatic embeddings, summarization, and relation extraction, but all core memory operations work. Error Handling and Retries All LLM adapter HTTP requests use exponential backoff with the backoff crate. Transient errors (network timeouts, 429 rate limits, 5xx server errors) are retried automatically. Permanent errors (401 unauthorized, 400 bad request) fail immediately. Next Steps

title: Contributing Guide
description: How to contribute to Cerememory: PR process, commit conventions, and code style.
content: Getting Started We welcome contributions to Cerememory. Before submitting a pull request, please read this guide to understand our workflow and expectations. Pull Request Process Fork and branch -- Fork the repository and create a feature branch from main: Make your changes -- Implement your feature or fix. Ensure all tests pass locally. Write tests -- New features require tests. Bug fixes should include a regression test. Aim for coverage of both success paths and failure modes. Submit a PR -- Open a pull request against main. Fill out the PR template with a clear description of the change and its motivation. Address review feedback -- Respond to review comments and push additional commits as needed. Commit Convention We use Conventional Commits with the following prefixes: | Prefix | Use Case | |--------|----------| | feat: | New feature or capability | | fix: | Bug fix | | refactor: | Code restructuring without behavior change | | test: | Adding or updating tests | | docs: | Documentation changes | | perf: | Performance improvement | | chore: | Build, CI, dependency updates | | breaking: | Breaking API change | Examples: Scope is optional but encouraged for crate-specific changes: Code Style Rust - Follow standard Rust idioms and the Rust API guidelines - Run cargo fmt before committing -- the CI enforces formatting - Run cargo clippy and address all warnings - Prefer impl Future over boxed futures for trait methods (except where dyn-compatibility requires boxing) - Use thiserror for error types; avoid anyhow in library crates (OK in CLI/binary crates) - Document public APIs with /// doc comments Testing - Use #[test] for unit tests and #[tokio::test] for async tests - Place unit tests in a mod tests at the bottom of each file - Integration tests go in the tests/integration crate - Use descriptive test names: fidelity_decreases_after_1h, not test_decay Documentation - MDX files use frontmatter with title and description - Use components: Callout, Tabs, Steps, Terminal, FileTree, LinkCard - Keep code examples accurate and runnable Branch Naming | Pattern | Example | |---------|---------| | feat/ | feat/timeline-query | | fix/ | fix/decay-negative-fidelity | | refactor/ | refactor/store-trait-simplify | | docs/ | docs/cmp-spec-update | Issue Labels | Label | Description | |-------|-------------| | bug | Something is broken | | enhancement | New feature request | | good first issue | Suitable for newcomers | | help wanted | Community contributions welcome | | breaking | Involves breaking API changes | | performance | Performance-related | License By contributing to Cerememory, you agree that your contributions will be licensed under the MIT License. Next Steps

title: Development Setup
description: Set up your local development environment for Cerememory development.
content: Prerequisites - Rust 1.88+ -- Install via rustup - Protobuf compiler -- Required for gRPC code generation - Node.js 18+ -- Required for TypeScript native bindings (optional) - Python 3.10+ -- Required for Python native bindings (optional) - maturin -- Required for building Python native bindings (optional) Clone and Build The first build downloads and compiles all dependencies. Subsequent incremental builds are fast. The workspace contains over 20 crates. Use cargo build -p cerememory-core to build a specific crate during focused development. Running Tests All Tests Specific Crate Integration Tests With Output Linting and Formatting Format Check Format Fix Clippy CI runs both cargo fmt --check and cargo clippy -D warnings. PRs that fail either check will not be merged. Development Workflow Running the Server Locally With an LLM provider: Testing with curl Running Benchmarks Project Structure Key directories for development: | Path | Purpose | |------|---------| | crates/cerememory-core/ | Types, traits, protocol -- start here for data model changes | | crates/cerememory-engine/ | Central engine -- start here for behavior changes | | crates/cerememory-decay/ | Decay math and engine -- isolated, easy to test | | crates/cerememory-transport-http/ | HTTP endpoints -- start here for API changes | | crates/cerememory-config/ | Configuration loading and validation | | crates/cerememory-store-/ | Individual store implementations | | adapters/adapter-/ | LLM provider adapters | | bindings/ | PyO3 and napi-rs native bindings | | tests/integration/ | End-to-end integration tests | Adding a New Store Operation Define the protocol types in cerememory-core/src/protocol.rs -- add request and response structs. Add the trait method to cerememory-core/src/traits.rs if the operation involves a new store capability. Implement in the engine in cerememory-engine/src/lib.rs. Add the HTTP handler in cerememory-transport-http/src/lib.rs -- add the route and handler function. Add the CLI command in cerememory-cli/src/main.rs -- add a new variant to the Commands enum. Write tests at each layer: unit tests in the crate, integration tests in tests/integration. Adding a New LLM Adapter 1. Create a new crate under adapters/adapter-/ 2. Implement the LLMProvider trait from cerememory-core 3. Add the crate to the workspace Cargo.toml 4. Add a feature flag to cerememory-cli (e.g., llm-) 5. Wire the provider in cerememory-cli/src/main.rs under build_llm_provider() Debugging Tips Enable Trace Logging Per-Crate Logging JSON Log Format For structured logging (useful with log aggregation tools): Next Steps
